evidence_quotes.quote: I don't like to see products being taken away from the customer (by the manufacturer) after they have been purchased.
These are purchases, not subscription plans.
It's similar to shipping an update for an electric car, which stops the owner from driving it, because there's a new model that the manufacturer would like the customer to buy instead.

paragraphs.text: As many others, I don't like to see products being taken away from the customer (by the manufacturer) after they have been purchased. This has been accepted as a marketing strategy for video games so far, maybe due to the medium being virtual, as well as having a 'playful' quality, but that doesn't change the fact that customers have bought these products, trusting that they were theirs to keep, as you would with any product you purchase. These are purchases, not subscription plans. It's similar to shipping an update for an electric car, which stops the owner from driving it, because there's a new model that the manufacturer would like the customer to buy instead. This scenario could likely be the outcome, if we allow this behaviour, which is why it regards all of us, not just the video game customers.
